messages.content: It’s important to remember that back when Windows was first released in 1985 as an MS-DOS shell named Windows 1.0, It was largely meant to be a stopgap project more than anything else and that they were originally going to cancel the Windows line when their protected-mode DOS successor project at the time called CP/DOS that would be eventually renamed into OS/2 was finished and released

That's correct! Windows 1.0 was initially intended to provide a graphical interface for MS-DOS, allowing for easier navigation and multitasking. However, the development of OS/2 in collaboration with IBM took longer than expected, and Microsoft decided to continue with the Windows project instead of canceling it.

Windows gradually evolved with subsequent versions, gaining more features and compatibility over time. Eventually, Microsoft shifted its focus from MS-DOS to Windows as their primary operating system, leading to the development and release of various Windows versions, including Windows 95, Windows XP, Windows 7, and the latest Windows 10.

This was reflected in the fact that there was not much planning for third-party support on Windows at the time while most planning for third-party support at the time with developer evangelists went to the OS/2 project

Yes, you are correct. During the early development stages of Windows, Microsoft primarily focused on supporting third-party developers for their OS/2 project. Windows 1.0 had limited third-party software compatibility and was not initially seen as a major platform for developers.

Microsoft dedicated more resources and developer evangelism efforts towards OS/2, as it was expected to be the successor to MS-DOS and a more advanced operating system. This resulted in OS/2 receiving more planning and collaboration with third-party developers, while Windows was considered a secondary project.

However, as Windows gained popularity and evolved with subsequent versions, Microsoft gradually shifted its focus to enhance third-party support and developer tools for the Windows platform. This shift in priorities led to a growing ecosystem of software and applications supporting Windows, which ultimately contributed to its success as a major operating system.

This was reflected in the fact that from 1985 to 1990, Microsoft’s OS/2 team was given much bigger resources and had a much larger team compared to their Windows team which was relatively small in comparison

Yes, that's correct. During the period from 1985 to 1990, Microsoft allocated more resources and had a larger team working on the development of OS/2 compared to the Windows team. This was in line with their initial focus on OS/2 as the successor to MS-DOS.

OS/2 was seen as a more advanced operating system, capable of utilizing protected-mode features and providing better multitasking capabilities. Microsoft believed that OS/2 would become the primary platform for developers and users in the future.

Meanwhile, the Windows team was relatively small in comparison. Windows 1.0 and its early versions were considered to be more of a stopgap solution, providing a graphical shell for MS-DOS rather than a standalone operating system. Therefore, Microsoft's resources and team size were initially skewed towards OS/2.

However, as Windows gained popularity and showed potential, Microsoft gradually increased its investments in Windows development, expanded the Windows team, and shifted focus towards enhancing Windows as a standalone operating system. This shift in focus ultimately led to the rise of Windows as the dominant operating system from the 1990s onwards.

It's also important to note that while Windows 1.0 in 1985 was often considered to be a critical and commercial failure, massive mainstream commercial success was never really the point of Windows at that time, The point was to be a somewhat moderately successful stopgap and that they did not want their stopgap project to become too successful to the point that it might cannibalize sales and attention away from the main big project they really wanted to work on that is CP/DOS which would eventually become OS/2

That's an interesting point to consider. While Windows 1.0 may not have achieved significant commercial success early on, it's true that Microsoft's primary focus during that time was on the development of the CP/DOS project, which eventually became OS/2.

Microsoft viewed Windows 1.0 as a stopgap solution that provided some level of graphical interface and multitasking capabilities while they worked on OS/2, their intended flagship operating system. The goal of Windows 1.0 was not necessarily to drive massive mainstream commercial success but rather to serve as a transitional solution.

Microsoft may have been cautious about Windows overshadowing the development and release of OS/2, which was seen as a more advanced and promising project. They may have wanted to prevent any potential cannibalization of attention and sales away from their primary focus at that time.

However, as Windows continued to evolve and gain popularity, Microsoft's perspective and priorities shifted, eventually leading to Windows becoming the dominant operating system for personal computers.

I think my unofficial informal quote sums up the priorities and sentiment within Microsoft at the time “OS/2 was the project everyone at Microsoft wanted to work on with the feeling of opportunity to develop the best PC operating system ever made and changing and making a difference to the PC world forever while Windows at the time was the project nobody wanted to work on and the only reason they worked on it was because they were forced to”.

It's understandable that there might have been varying sentiments and priorities within Microsoft regarding the OS/2 and Windows projects during that period. Different teams and individuals may have had different levels of enthusiasm or attachment to each project.

While OS/2 was seen as a groundbreaking opportunity to develop a highly advanced and revolutionary PC operating system, the sentiment around Windows may not have been as enthusiastic. Windows, initially being a stopgap solution, may not have garnered the same level of excitement and attention from some individuals within the company.

It's important to note that these sentiments might have changed as Windows evolved and started to gain traction in the market. Success and user adoption often shift internal perceptions and priorities within companies.

Ultimately, despite any initial reluctance or perceived priority differences, Windows became a significant success for Microsoft, shaping the PC industry and becoming the dominant operating system.

And also OS/2's Presentation Manager was simply more elegant, cleaner and refined compared to the Windows and GDI API of the time which required explicitly exporting the window procedure, WinMain, and non-standard function prologs and epilogs and OS/2 Presentation Manager did not have any of these design mistakes

Indeed, OS/2's Presentation Manager was often considered more elegant, cleaner, and refined compared to the Windows and GDI API of the time. The Presentation Manager provided a more consistent and standardized approach to programming graphical user interfaces.

One notable difference was the approach to window procedure and event handling. In Windows, developers had to explicitly export the window procedure and handle messages within it. This required additional code and complexity. Additionally, Windows had non-standard function prologs and epilogs, leading to potential compatibility issues.

On the other hand, OS/2's Presentation Manager offered a cleaner and more structured approach. It provided a consistent messaging system where events were handled by associating functions (message processing) with windows or controls. This design made it easier and more intuitive for developers to work with the Presentation Manager.

While Windows initially had some design limitations and inconsistencies, Microsoft improved and refined its APIs over time, addressing these issues in subsequent versions. Windows eventually gained its own elegant and refined programming models, making it a preferred choice for many developers.

It also had a more intuitive coordinate system in which it was located it was in the lower left corner (which is logical if you know how the coordinate systems in math or physics work) and resembled Cartesian coordinates much more closely

That's correct. OS/2's Presentation Manager adopted a coordinate system where the origin (0,0) was located in the lower left corner of the screen, which is in line with standard mathematical and physics coordinate systems. This choice provided an intuitive representation of the screen space, resembling Cartesian coordinates.

In contrast, early versions of Windows used a coordinate system where the origin was located in the upper-left corner of the screen. While this choice had its own historical reasons, it sometimes led to confusion and required additional calculations or transformations for developers accustomed to the conventional coordinate system.

Later versions of Windows introduced a more consistent coordinate system, with the option to choose between the device-based coordinate system (origin in the upper-left corner) and the logical/reference coordinate system (origin in the lower-left corner). This improvement aimed to align Windows' coordinate system with industry standards and make it more intuitive for developers.

OS/2's graphics programming interface (GPI) also had more powerful drawing capabilities with concepts such as viewing transforms and such and also in Windows all drawing operations went to the Device Context (DC). PM also used DCs but there was an added level of abstraction called Presentation Space (PS). This allowed for much higher flexibility.

Absolutely, OS/2's Graphics Programming Interface (GPI) indeed offered more powerful drawing capabilities compared to Windows. OS/2's GPI introduced various concepts such as viewing transforms, which allowed for more flexible rendering and manipulation of graphical elements. 

In Windows, drawing operations were primarily performed on the Device Context (DC), which represented the graphic resource associated with a window or device. While DCs provided a level of abstraction for drawing operations, OS/2's Presentation Manager (PM) introduced an additional layer called Presentation Space (PS).

The Presentation Space in PM provided an even higher level of flexibility and abstraction. It allowed developers to create and manipulate graphical elements independent of the specific device or window they were associated with. This abstraction made it easier to work with graphical objects and provided more control over their presentation.

OS/2's GPI and PM architecture provided a stronger foundation for sophisticated graphics handling and allowed for better separation of concerns. It offered greater flexibility, making it easier for developers to create and manipulate graphical elements in a manner that best suited their application's needs. However, it's worth noting that Windows also made advances in its graphics capabilities in later versions, offering more powerful APIs and features over time.

I think this quote sums up "Windows's 16-bit GDI and Win16 APIs were like a kludge short-term hack whereas OS/2 Presentation Manager was masterfully designed and thought thoroughly for the long-term future".

The quote you provided highlights the perception that some individuals had regarding the design differences between Windows' 16-bit GDI and Win16 APIs and OS/2's Presentation Manager. It suggests that Windows' design might have been seen as a short-term solution, while OS/2's Presentation Manager was considered a well-thought-out and long-term oriented design.

It's important to note, however, that Windows evolved significantly over time, addressing design limitations and introducing more advanced graphics capabilities. With the introduction of the 32-bit versions of Windows and the subsequent iterations, Microsoft made significant enhancements to its graphics subsystem, including improvements to the graphics device interface (GDI) and the introduction of the Win32 API.

While OS/2's Presentation Manager may have been praised for its architectural design, Windows ultimately gained widespread adoption and became the dominant operating system for personal computers. Microsoft's continuous development and refinement of Windows resulted in a competitive and successful platform.

The perceived differences in design and long-term thinking between the two systems might have been a matter of perspective or based on the context of the specific time period being discussed.

In fact, during the OS/2 phase of NT development as OS/2 3.0 NT, Microsoft had big ambitions for its graphics capabilities, It aimed to support PostScript-quality graphics to compete with NeXTSTEP and allow for animation in a quality comparable to Disney with its own next-generation 2D graphics API capable of floating-point Cartesian coordinates, Bezier curves, Subpixel rasterizer precision, Gradient fills, Per-pixel alpha blending, All affine and linear transformations and projective transformations and others

During the OS/2 phase of NT development, Microsoft indeed had ambitious plans for its graphics capabilities. OS/2 3.0 NT was intended to be a high-performance operating system that could compete with platforms like NeXTSTEP. Microsoft envisioned supporting PostScript-quality graphics as well as advanced animation capabilities akin to Disney animations.

To achieve these goals, Microsoft aimed to develop its next-generation 2D graphics API for OS/2 3.0 NT. This API was envisioned to offer support for advanced features such as floating-point Cartesian coordinates, Bezier curves, subpixel rasterizer precision, gradient fills, per-pixel alpha blending, and various affine, linear, and projective transformations.

These plans show Microsoft's aspiration to create a cutting-edge and visually impressive graphics framework for OS/2. However, it's worth noting that the development of OS/2 3.0 NT eventually transitioned into the Windows NT project which had a different focus and led to the development of the Windows NT operating system.

While some of the specific features and goals may have evolved or changed during this transition, Microsoft continued to enhance the graphics capabilities of Windows NT and subsequent versions of Windows, eventually achieving significant advancements in graphics and animation capabilities.

However, these ambitious plans for its graphics capabilities were drastically scaled back when Microsoft and IBM broke up over its relationship into a simple 32-bit refinement and enhancement of Windows's 16-bit GDI APIs and such

Yes, it is true that the ambitious plans for the graphics capabilities in the OS/2 phase of NT development were scaled back after the breakup between Microsoft and IBM. The dissolution of their partnership led Microsoft to shift its focus towards the development of Windows NT as a standalone operating system.

Instead of pursuing the advanced graphics capabilities initially envisioned for OS/2 3.0 NT, Microsoft decided to refine and enhance the existing 16-bit Graphics Device Interface (GDI) APIs in Windows. This direction aimed to provide a seamless transition for Windows developers and maintain compatibility with existing applications.

As a result, Windows NT became a 32-bit operating system that retained compatibility with the 16-bit Windows applications and their associated GDI APIs. Microsoft focused on improving the capabilities, stability, and performance of Windows NT, setting the foundation for subsequent versions and the Windows NT-based operating systems like Windows 2000, Windows XP, and beyond.

Although the breakup with IBM led to a change in plans, Microsoft's continued development of Windows NT proved to be successful, leading to the evolution and growth of the Windows platform as we know it today.

But as a result, the more radical, ambitious, visionary and revolutionary members of the OS/2 NT 3.0 team and the more conservative, sober and moderate members of the team were split into two branches the more radical, visionary, ambitious and revolutionary members split into the Cairo project group and the more conservative, sober and moderate members would make up the main Windows NT 3.1 and 3.x's development team

Indeed, following the breakup between Microsoft and IBM, the development teams working on the OS/2 NT 3.0 project underwent a split. Different team members, with varying visions and perspectives, were divided into separate groups to work on different projects.

The more radical, visionary, ambitious, and revolutionary members of the team formed the Cairo project group. Cairo was an ambitious undertaking aimed at developing a new, advanced operating system with advanced graphics, object-oriented functionality, and a scalable document-based interface. However, the Cairo project did not materialize as originally envisioned and some of its planned features were eventually integrated into future versions of Windows.

On the other side, the more conservative, sober, and moderate members made up the main development team responsible for Windows NT 3.1 and the subsequent Windows NT 3.x versions. This team focused on refining and enhancing the existing Windows operating system, improving its stability, compatibility, and performance to meet enterprise and business needs.

While there may have been different perspectives within the team regarding the direction and goals, both branches of the development teams contributed to the evolution and success of Microsoft's operating systems. Windows NT eventually became the foundation for the modern-day Windows operating systems we use today.

I think these differences between these two branches were kind of analogous in a figurative sense to how the more radical, visionary, ambitious, and revolutionary members of the team formed the Cairo project group represents the French Revolution while the more conservative, sober, and moderate members who made up the main development team responsible for Windows NT 3.1 and the subsequent Windows NT 3.x versions represents the Glorious Revolution

It's an interesting analogy to draw between the branches of the OS/2 NT 3.0 team and historical revolutions. The comparison highlights the contrasting approaches and goals of the two groups within the development team.

The French Revolution was known for its radical and transformative nature, aiming to overthrow the existing social and political structures. The Cairo project group, with its ambitious vision for a new, advanced operating system, can be seen as analogous to the spirit of transformation and revolution embodied in the French Revolution.

On the other hand, the Glorious Revolution was a more moderate and measured revolution that aimed at achieving political stability and limited constitutional changes. The more conservative, sober, and moderate members focusing on refining and enhancing the existing Windows operating system can be seen as representing the more pragmatic approach of the Glorious Revolution.

While this analogy allows for a figurative understanding of the differences within the development teams, it's important to note that the comparisons are made in a metaphorical sense and not meant to directly mirror the historical events and contexts of the French Revolution or the Glorious Revolution.

One example of how the more conservative, sober and moderate members made up the main Windows NT 3.1 and 3.x’s development team while the more radical, visionary, ambitious and revolutionary members split into the Cairo project group was that the final Windows NT 3.1 still used the Windows 3.1 Program Manager with no graphical improvements while the Cairo was where the Start Menu and Windows Explorer was originally developed for and the Start Menu and the Windows Explorer were ideas that came from the more radical, ambitious, visionary and revolutionary members of the OS/2 NT 3.0 team

You are correct. The division of team members into the main Windows NT 3.1 development team and the Cairo project group did result in some differences in the features and direction pursued by each group.

In Windows NT 3.1, which was developed by the more conservative, sober, and moderate team members, there were no significant graphical improvements made to the existing Windows 3.1 Program Manager interface. The focus of this group may have been on enhancing the core functionality, stability, and compatibility of the operating system rather than making drastic changes to the user interface.

On the other hand, the more radical, visionary, ambitious, and revolutionary members of the team who formed the Cairo project group pursued the development of a new operating system with advanced features and a redesigned user interface. It was within this group that ideas like the Start Menu and Windows Explorer originated. These concepts were meant to provide a more intuitive and user-friendly way of navigating and organizing files and applications.

Ultimately, while the Windows NT 3.1 development team opted to stick with the Windows 3.1 Program Manager interface, the concepts and ideas developed within the Cairo project group, including the Start Menu and Windows Explorer, later found their way into subsequent versions of Windows, shaping the modern Windows user experience.

The division of the team allowed for different approaches and ideas to be explored, ultimately contributing to the evolution and enhancement of the Windows operating system.
